topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an oppressive monotony, mirroring the churning in my stomach. I stared at the printed confirmation email, the subject line a cruel joke: "Interview Invitation - Marketing Coordinator." My jaw tightened so hard I thought my molars might crack. Liam. Of course. He’d managed to snag the position I’d been meticulously preparing for, the one I’d poured months of late nights into mastering. I slammed the folder shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the otherwise quiet cubicle farm. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a tight, hot pressure behind my eyes. "Of course," I muttered to the empty air, my voice sounding rougher than I intended. It felt like a complete, utter injustice, a deliberate slight.

[story 2]

I paced the length of my small apartment, the worn floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. Every time I glanced at the screen displaying Liam’s acceptance notification, a sharp, unpleasant clenching sensation gripped my chest. My hands were clenched into fists so tight my knuckles were white, the skin pulling taut over the bone. I stopped abruptly, running both hands through my hair, pulling at the strands in a frantic gesture. "This is ridiculous," I hissed, the words tasting metallic on my tongue. I reread the job description, the very details I had excelled at, and a bitter taste flooded my mouth. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a raw, burning frustration that made my breath catch in my throat. I wanted to shout, to dismantle the entire system that allowed this kind of unfairness to happen.

[story 3]

When the notification finally appeared, I didn't move immediately. I just sat down heavily on the edge of my desk, the cheap plastic groaning under the sudden weight. A slow, deep exhale escaped me, shaky and uneven. Then, the controlled composure I usually maintained fractured. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over Liam’s contact, a muscle ticking violently in my jaw. "Are you serious?" I managed, the volume of my voice dangerously low, laced with a brittle edge that made my own ears ring. I pushed the phone away as if it were physically contaminated. The sheer audacity of it—the casual way he had secured what I had fought for—sent a wave of intense agitation washing over me, leaving me feeling shaky and utterly unbalanced.
